book_claims.claim: Quinoa should only be consumed if it was part of your ancestral diet; otherwise best avoided or used only occasionally
book_claims.mechanism: Risk of intolerances or immune reactions from non-ancestral foods; saponins and certain proteins may act as antigens

book_claims.claim: Quinoa increased the immune response in mice, suggesting potential to cause autoimmune diseases in humans, particularly those without quinoa in their ancestral diet
book_claims.mechanism: Quinoa contains saponins and certain proteins that may act as antigens and trigger immune responses in non-adapted individuals

supplementary_research.finding: Quinoa (PMID 25138217) provides a complete amino acid profile and high fiber content, associated with reduced LDL cholesterol and improved glycemic control in clinical studies
Saponin content in quinoa (PMID 29580532) can be largely removed by washing; rinsed quinoa shows minimal pro-inflammatory effect in humans, contrasting the book's caution
Quinoa is a gluten-free pseudocereal rich in protein (all essential amino acids), magnesium, iron, and antioxidant flavonoids; generally regarded as a healthful whole grain for most adults

texture: small beads with visible germ ring

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: High digestibility; DIAAS score superior to most plant proteins
Mix of soluble and insoluble fiber; supports gut microbiome
Phytate reduces absorption; soaking/rinsing improves bioavailability
Enhanced by pairing with vitamin C sources

synergies.description: Vitamin C in lemon enhances non-heme iron absorption from quinoa
Combined amino acid profiles provide complete protein with complementary fiber types
Fat improves absorption of fat-soluble antioxidants in quinoa
